insight: Master Bot struggles to respond correctly to aggressive Animus-based openings (e.g., A/11/11/11/11 as P1) in Base+Immolite sets, allowing this line to be exploited for the 'Kappa' achievement badge.
author: shadourow
context: Discussion about which badges are hardest to earn and how to exploit MB for the Kappa badge.

insight: Master Bot sometimes defends and then buys a Cluster Bolt on the same turn, creating a situation where the opponent can achieve an exact breach on a Centurion — a clear tactical error by the bot.
author: masn6811
context: Casual observation about a specific Master Bot blunder involving defense and Cluster Bolt purchase sequencing.

insight: In Red Base (Animus + Tarsier only, no Drones or Engineers), Master Bot as P2 appears much harder to beat than as P1, suggesting the bot plays near-optimally in that specific constrained setup when it has the second-player advantage, but human P2 likely wins with a fastimus line against MB as P1.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion of hyreon's attempts to beat Master Bot in Red Base only as P1, with amalloy and hyreon analyzing P1/P2 asymmetry.

insight: In Tarsimus (base set restricted to Animus and Tarsier with starting units, no Drones or Engineers), a human P2 can reliably beat Master Bot using a fastimus opening, exploiting the severe first-player advantage that exists in this restricted unit set.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion of whether Master Bot's performance in constrained sets proves game-theoretic outcomes; amalloy notes MB can lose these setups.

insight: Tia Threnody's presence in a set obliges both players to play more cautiously; the player who was already winning tends to buy it and accelerate their win, rather than Tia being an independent win condition that dominates regardless of game state.
author: amalloy
context: Debate over whether Tia Threnody is overpowered and auto-wins for whoever buys it first; amalloy and awaclus push back on this framing.

insight: Deliberately holding attack at exactly 4 damage (when the opponent has only blue tech) can be more effective than clicking Tantalum Ray to reach 5, because 4 damage forces the opponent into an awkward choice between defending Wall onto Wall or holding two Drones, whereas 5 damage lets them defend cleanly.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy explaining a nuanced case where activating Tantalum Ray is actually a mistake due to removing a tactical exploit.

insight: A Savior polywall requires 5 Drones to construct but needs 6 attackers to destroy, giving a net defensive advantage; however, selling the 5 Drones to build the polywall effectively also loses the Blastforge used to build them, making the true cost higher than it appears.
author: apooche
context: apooche analyzing the actual cost and value of constructing a Savior polywall, noting the hidden Blastforge cost.

insight: Deadeye Operative is most valuable in high-econ games where limiting the opponent's Drone supply matters, or in low-econ games as a rare counter to a red rush by depleting attacker Drones, and also functions as a Blue resource sink.
author: shadourow
context: Discussion of when Deadeye Operative is worth buying versus simply massing Drones, in the context of infinite-supply theoretical scenarios.

insight: Savior dramatically increases the value of Drones by allowing them to be cashed in defensively; with unlimited Savior supply, massing Drones becomes potentially broken because the usual supply constraint on Savior is removed.
author: vargus2254870
context: Theoretical discussion of infinite-supply scenarios, with shadourow and velizar_ agreeing that Savior makes Drones disproportionately strong.

insight: Removing units from the available set increases player imbalance, because fewer options make it more likely there is a single correct timing or line that is accessible to only one player.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy responding to hyreon's Red Base experiments, explaining why restricted sets amplify first- or second-player advantages.
